module: Module 6: Graph Sketching, Optimization & Implicit Behavior

sections.text: AP Calculus AB. Module Six — graph sketching, optimization, and implicit behavior. Today we turn the derivative theory from Module Five into the tools the AP exam loves to test: reading derivative graphs, optimizing real-world quantities, and handling curves that aren't even functions.

Here's the setup. You're designing a cylindrical soup can that has to hold exactly three hundred and fifty milliliters. What radius and what height use the least aluminum? You can't guess. You can't trial-and-error. There's exactly one right answer, and calculus pins it down. This is the kind of question calculus was actually built to solve.

Three jobs today. One — read graphs of f, f prime, and f double prime fluently, and sketch one from the other. Two — apply a clean five-step optimization method to any applied problem. Three — analyze implicit relations, curves like circles that aren't functions of x at all.

Here's the core translation. When f prime of x is positive, f is increasing. When f prime is negative, f is decreasing. Where f prime changes sign at a critical point, f has a relative extremum — a max if f prime goes plus to minus, a min if it goes minus to plus. That's the whole game with the first derivative.

Second derivative does concavity. When f double prime is positive, f is concave up — shaped like a smile, holding water. When f double prime is negative, f is concave down — shaped like a frown, spilling water. Where f double prime changes sign, you get a point of inflection — the curve's concavity flips.

Big warning first. The graph you're looking at is f prime, NOT f. Don't trace it as if it's f itself. Its y-values are slopes of f, not heights of f. Now read it. f prime is zero at x equals negative one and x equals three — those are critical points. Where f prime is above the axis, f is increasing. Where f prime is below, f is decreasing. So f goes up, hits a max at x equals negative one, comes down, hits a min at x equals three, then turns back up. That's the sketch.

Five steps every time. Step one — read the problem and draw the picture. Step two — identify the objective function and the constraint. Step three — reduce to one variable and write the feasibility check; ask what is the domain of that variable. Step four — differentiate and find the critical points. Step five — verify max versus min using the second derivative test or the closed-interval candidates test against the feasible endpoints. Skip step five and you'll lose points on the AP free response.

Inscribe a rectangle with base on the x-axis, top corners on the parabola y equals four minus x squared. Feasibility check first — we need four minus x squared at least zero, and x at least zero, so x lives in the closed interval from zero to two. The area is A of x equals two x times four minus x squared. Differentiate, set to zero, you get x equals the square root of four-thirds. Now run the candidates test on the feasibility interval. At x equals zero, A is zero. At x equals two, A is zero. At the interior critical point, A is approximately six point one six. The interior critical point wins — that's our maximum.

Always verify. The second derivative test or the closed-interval candidates test confirms which critical point is actually the maximum or minimum you want. On a closed feasibility interval, evaluate the objective at both endpoints and at every interior critical point, then compare — the largest value is the absolute maximum, the smallest is the absolute minimum. Don't just stop at the critical point and hope.
Your turn. A rectangle has perimeter forty. Find the dimensions that maximize its area. Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you're ready.
Your turn. A rectangle has perimeter forty. Find the dimensions that maximize its area. Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you're ready.

Walking through it. Constraint: two L plus two W equals forty, so W equals twenty minus L. Objective: A of L equals L times twenty minus L. Differentiate — A prime equals twenty minus two L. Set to zero — L equals ten. So W is also ten. The answer is a ten by ten square. Symmetric answers like this are very common in optimization — when the constraint is symmetric, the optimum usually is too.

Some curves aren't functions of x. The circle x squared plus y squared equals twenty-five fails the vertical line test — for most x values, there are two y values. But we can still get a slope. Implicit differentiation treats y as a function of x, applies the chain rule, then solves for dy dx. The slope expression involves both x and y, and that's fine.

On the circle x squared plus y squared equals twenty-five, differentiate both sides with respect to x. You get two x plus two y times dy dx equals zero. Solve — dy dx equals negative x over y. Horizontal tangents happen where dy dx is zero, so where x equals zero — that's the points zero comma five and zero comma negative five. Vertical tangents happen where dy dx is undefined, so where y equals zero — that's the points five comma zero and negative five comma zero. Exactly the top, bottom, and sides of the circle, which matches your intuition.

The number one AP optimization mistake — a student finds the critical point, plugs it in, reports the answer, and stops. But they never checked whether it's a max or a min. On free response, that costs you the verification point even if your number is right. The fix is the verify step. Second derivative test or candidates test, every single time.

Six takeaways. Sign of f prime gives monotonicity. Sign of f double prime gives concavity. To sketch f from f prime, read where it's positive, negative, and where it changes sign. Optimize with the five-step method including the feasibility check. Always verify whether a critical point is a max or a min. And implicit differentiation handles non-function curves like circles.

This video is the overview, not the full module. To master it — one, read OpenStax Calculus Volume One, chapters four-point-seven through four-point-eight. Two, work the Khan Academy Unit Five Part Two problems on optimization and analyzing derivative graphs. Three, complete the GIIS optimization problem set in your dashboard. Four, book a fifteen-minute advisor check-in to walk through an optimization free-response together. Next up — Module Seven: Riemann sums and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, where we move from differentiation to integration.
